subUnits.title: 5.1 Definition and Components of the Solar System
notes: The Solar System:
• The solar system is the collection of celestial bodies that orbit around the Sun
• It consists of the Sun, eight planets, their moons, asteroids, comets, meteoroids, and other celestial objects
• The Sun is at the center and provides light and heat energy
• All planets move around the Sun in elliptical orbits (paths)
Components of the Solar System:
1. The Sun:
• A star at the center of the solar system
• Diameter: approximately 1.4 million km
• Temperature: about 5,500°C at surface, 15 million°C at core
• Provides light and heat energy for life on Earth
• Makes up 99.8% of the solar system's mass
2. The Planets (in order from the Sun):
a) Mercury: Closest to Sun, smallest planet, no atmosphere, extreme temperatures
b) Venus: Similar size to Earth, thick atmosphere, hottest planet
c) Earth: Only planet with life, has water and atmosphere
d) Mars: Red planet, has polar ice caps, potential for past life
e) Jupiter: Largest planet, has Great Red Spot, many moons
f) Saturn: Known for its rings, second largest planet
g) Uranus: Rotates on its side, blue-green color
h) Neptune: Farthest planet, strong winds, blue color
3. Natural Satellites (Moons):
• Bodies that orbit around planets
• Earth has one moon; Jupiter has over 80 moons
• Moons reflect sunlight and affect tides on Earth
4. Asteroids:
• Small rocky bodies orbiting the Sun
• Most found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter
• Range in size from dust particles to dwarf planets
5. Comets:
• Icy bodies that develop tails when near the Sun
• Have elliptical orbits that take them far from the Sun
• Example: Halley's Comet (visible every 76 years)
6. Meteoroids, Meteors, and Meteorites:
• Meteoroids: Small rocky/metallic bodies in space
• Meteors: Meteoroids that burn up in Earth's atmosphere (shooting stars)
• Meteorites: Meteoroids that reach Earth's surface
7. Dwarf Planets:
• Pluto, Eris, Ceres, Haumea, Makemake
• Orbit the Sun but haven't cleared their orbital paths

quiz.q: The solar system consists of:
opts: The Sun, planets, moons, and other celestial bodies
quiz.explanation: The solar system includes the Sun, eight planets, their moons, asteroids, comets, meteoroids, and other celestial objects orbiting the Sun.

quiz.q: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
quiz.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun, orbiting at an average distance of about 58 million km.

quiz.q: The largest planet in the solar system is:
quiz.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet, with a diameter of about 143,000 km and mass 318 times that of Earth.

quiz.q: Which planet is known as the 'Red Planet'?
quiz.explanation: Mars is called the Red Planet due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface giving it a reddish appearance.

quiz.q: The asteroid belt is located between:
quiz.explanation: The main asteroid belt is located between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter, containing millions of rocky bodies.

quiz.q: Comets develop tails when they:
quiz.explanation: Comets develop glowing tails when they approach the Sun; solar heat vaporizes their icy components.

quiz.q: A meteor that reaches Earth's surface is called a:
quiz.explanation: Meteoroids are in space; meteors burn in atmosphere; meteorites are those that survive to reach Earth's surface.

quiz.q: Which planet has the most prominent ring system?
quiz.explanation: Saturn has the most extensive and visible ring system, made of ice particles, rock, and dust.

quiz.q: The Sun makes up approximately what percentage of the solar system's mass?
quiz.explanation: The Sun contains about 99.8% of the total mass of the solar system, with planets and other bodies making up the remainder.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a dwarf planet?
quiz.explanation: Mars is a full-sized planet. Dwarf planets include Pluto, Eris, Ceres, Haumea, and Makemake.

subUnits.title: 5.2 Size and Shape of the Earth
notes: Shape of the Earth:
• The Earth is not a perfect sphere but an oblate spheroid (geoid)
• Slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator
• Equatorial diameter: 12,756 km
• Polar diameter: 12,714 km
• Difference: 42 km (equatorial bulge)
Evidence for Earth's Sphericity:
1. Circumnavigation:
• Ships and aircraft can travel around the Earth and return to starting point
• Ferdinand Magellan's expedition (1519-1522) first circumnavigation
2. Lunar Eclipse:
• Earth's shadow on the Moon during lunar eclipse is always circular
• Only a spherical object casts a circular shadow from all angles
3. Ship's Appearance on Horizon:
• Ships appear to sink below horizon as they sail away
• Mast appears last when ship approaches; hull disappears first when leaving
• Due to Earth's curvature
4. Aerial and Satellite Photographs:
• Photos from space clearly show Earth as a sphere
• Modern technology provides direct visual evidence
5. Variation in Star Positions:
• Different stars visible from different latitudes
• Polaris (North Star) visible only in Northern Hemisphere
• Due to Earth's curved surface
6. Sunrise and Sunset Times:
• Sun rises and sets at different times in different longitudes
• Due to Earth's rotation and spherical shape
Size of the Earth:
• Equatorial circumference: 40,075 km
• Polar circumference: 40,008 km
• Surface area: 510 million km²
• Volume: 1.083 billion km³
• Mass: 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg
• Average density: 5.52 g/cm³
Importance of Earth's Shape and Size:
• Affects climate patterns and seasons
• Influences navigation and mapping
• Determines gravitational force
• Affects satellite orbits and communications
• Essential for understanding global phenomena

quiz.q: The Earth's shape is best described as:
quiz.explanation: Earth is an oblate spheroid (geoid), slightly flattened at poles and bulging at equator due to rotation.

quiz.q: The difference between Earth's equatorial and polar diameter is approximately:
quiz.explanation: Equatorial diameter is 12,756 km; polar diameter is 12,714 km; difference is 42 km due to rotational bulge.

quiz.q: Evidence for Earth's sphericity from lunar eclipse is that:
opts: Earth's shadow on Moon is always circular
quiz.explanation: Only a spherical object casts a circular shadow from all angles; Earth's shadow during lunar eclipse is always circular.

quiz.q: When a ship sails away, the hull disappears before the mast because:
quiz.explanation: Due to Earth's curved surface, the hull disappears below the horizon first, followed by the mast.

quiz.q: The equatorial circumference of Earth is approximately:
quiz.explanation: Earth's equatorial circumference is approximately 40,075 km, while polar circumference is slightly less at 40,008 km.

quiz.q: Which observation proves Earth is spherical?
opts: Different stars visible at different latitudes
quiz.explanation: Different stars are visible from different latitudes because Earth's curved surface blocks different parts of the sky.

quiz.q: The first successful circumnavigation of Earth was completed by:
quiz.explanation: Magellan's expedition (1519-1522) was the first to circumnavigate Earth, proving it was spherical.

quiz.q: Earth's surface area is approximately:
quiz.explanation: Earth's total surface area is approximately 510 million square kilometers, with about 71% covered by water.

quiz.q: The Earth's average density is:
quiz.explanation: Earth's average density is 5.52 g/cm³, higher than surface rocks due to dense core materials.

quiz.q: Modern direct evidence of Earth's shape comes from:
quiz.explanation: Satellite and space photographs provide direct visual evidence of Earth's spherical shape.
